narrative_blocks.header: She saved Mark Twain's house from demolition
narrative_blocks.body: Day grew up playing in Mark Twain's Hartford house, where her grandparents John and Isabella Hooker lived next door. In 1929, at fifty-seven, she led the campaign that rescued the house from the wrecking ball.

Day later founded the Stowe-Day Foundation, today the Harriet Beecher Stowe Center, to preserve her family's literary heritage. She was a grandniece of Harriet Beecher Stowe and granddaughter of Isabella Beecher Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: She earned graduate degrees past fifty
narrative_blocks.body: Day studied painting under William Merritt Chase, then took a Radcliffe master's in psychology and a Trinity College master's in history. She came to her preservation work as a serious scholar.

narrative_blocks.header: She took on Tammany Hall in New York
narrative_blocks.body: Before her Hartford years, Day fought New York's Tammany Hall machine through the Women's Municipal League. The reform instinct ran in her Beecher blood.

cross_connections.display_label: As a child, Katharine played with Twain's daughters in his Farmington Avenue house; her family later leased it for two years; she saved it from demolition in 1929.
Stowe was Katharine's great-grandaunt — Katharine purchased her Hartford house in 1924 and spent her life preserving it.
Isabella Beecher Hooker was Katharine's grandmother — she grew up on Nook Farm watching Isabella's suffragist work, and the foundation she created bore Isabella's name.
John Hooker was Katharine's grandfather — he co-founded Nook Farm and practiced law at 96 Main Street Farmington alongside Sarah Porter; Katharine preserved the community he built.
led Litchfield's preservation as Day did Hartford's

parents.father_research_notes: Calvin Day (b.1852 house on Spring Street Hartford, dry goods merchant)

notable.notable_blurb: Connecticut abolitionist who bought Pennington's freedom

cross_connections.display_label: Practiced law where Sarah Porter first taught her students
cross_connections.description: Hooker practiced law in the stone store at 96 Main Street Farmington in 1843, the same building where Sarah Porter taught her first students as she established her school.

cross_connections.display_label: Co-founded Nook Farm; Mark Twain moved in 1874
cross_connections.description: John Hooker helped found the Nook Farm community on Farmington Avenue Hartford; Mark Twain moved in as neighbor in 1874.

cross_connections.display_label: His granddaughter preserved the Nook Farm community
cross_connections.description: Katharine Seymour Day was John Hooker's granddaughter — she grew up on Nook Farm, which he had co-founded, and spent her life preserving the community he created.

cross_connections.display_label: co-founded Nook Farm and sat in the US Senate
cross_connections.description: Co-founded Nook Farm together in 1853 — Hooker and Gillette bought 140 Hartford acres and built the literary community.

cross_connections.display_label: Iowa land they bought together launched Gillette's career
cross_connections.description: Gillette went to Iowa to manage land Hooker and his own father had purchased together — a Hartford investment that launched a Greenback congressman.

cross_connections.display_label: Bought Pennington's freedom from slavery in 1851
cross_connections.description: When the 1850 Fugitive Slave Law endangered Pennington — his friend and parishioner — Hooker opened secret negotiations with Frisby Tilghman's heirs. Hooker briefly took legal 'ownership' of Pennington to manumit him in New York; British abolitionists raised the purchase funds.

cross_connections.display_label: Married daughter of Rev. Lyman Beecher, recruited by this jurist.
Fellow Connecticut abolitionist working through law and Underground Railroad.
Fellow Hooker-descendant Connecticut abolitionist of the same generation.
Fellow Hooker-descendant abolitionist of the antebellum Northern circuit.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded Nook Farm; invited Mark Twain next door
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker was a Hartford lawyer who, with Francis Gillette, co-purchased the Nook Farm land in 1853 and developed it into the literary colony where his wife Isabella Beecher Hooker, Harriet Beecher Stowe, and eventually Mark Twain lived as neighbors. He was also a supporter of women's suffrage, notably influenced by watching his wife Isabella dedicate her life to it after reading Blackstone in his law office.

narrative_blocks.header: Bought Rev. Pennington's freedom from slavery, 1851
narrative_blocks.body: After the 1850 Fugitive Slave Law endangered formerly-enslaved people living in the North, John Hooker legally purchased Rev. James Pennington's freedom from his former owner's estate in 1851. Pennington had escaped Maryland slavery, attended Yale, become a Congregational minister, and authored 'The Fugitive Blacksmith' (1849).

narrative_blocks.header: Published the Charter Oak abolitionist newspaper, 1838
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker published the Charter Oak with the Connecticut Anti-Slavery Society of Hartford. Its motto: 'Free Principles — Free Men — Free Speech — And A Free Press.' Digital copies survive at the Connecticut State Library's Newspapers of Connecticut collection.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-wrote Connecticut's Married Women's Property Act
narrative_blocks.body: John and Isabella jointly wrote 'A Woman's Property Bill,' which became Connecticut's Married Women's Property Act of 1877. The act let a married woman control her own property and sue or be sued the same as her husband — undoing the coverture rules John had read about in Blackstone.

narrative_blocks.header: Reporter of Connecticut Supreme Court thirty-six years
narrative_blocks.body: In 1858 John Hooker became Reporter of the Connecticut Supreme Court of Errors — a role he held for thirty-six years until his 1894 retirement. He reported thirty-eight volumes of Connecticut Reports, the lasting professional achievement that defined his second career. The only longer-serving Connecticut reporter was Thomas Day (fifty-one years, 1802-1853), who had reported twenty-six volumes.

career.role: Lawyer + Clerk of the Connecticut Supreme Court
career.notes: Practicing lawyer in Hartford; long-tenured Clerk of the CT Supreme Court.

John and Isabella moved from Farmington to Hartford in early 1850s and helped establish the Nook Farm community with the Gillettes (his sister Elisabeth Hooker Gillette and her husband Francis Gillette). Mark Twain became a Nook Farm neighbor in 1874.

career.organization: Deming's Store, Mill Lane, Farmington CT
career.notes: John rented an office at Deming's Store directly next door to a room used by the Africans from the Amistad during their stay in Farmington.

career.role: Legal purchase of Rev. James Pennington's freedom
career.notes: After the 1850 Fugitive Slave Law, John legally purchased Rev. James Pennington's freedom from his former owner's estate. Pennington — formerly enslaved in Maryland, escaped to Connecticut, attended Yale, and became a Congregational minister — wrote 'The Fugitive Blacksmith' (1849).

career.role: CT women's property rights legislative campaign
career.notes: Influenced by his wife Isabella, John presented a bill making husbands and wives equal in property rights; passed Connecticut General Assembly 1877.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Inherited owner (1846-1864); Inherited from his father Edward Hooker (H00630) in 1846. John and Isabella lived here until early 1850s when they moved to Hartford to establish Nook Farm. House remained in family until sold 1864.
Co-founder (1853-1901); Co-founded with brother-in-law Francis Gillette (140 acres, Farmington Avenue).

parents.mother_research_notes: Harriet Porter Beecher (2nd wife of Lyman)

cross_connections.display_label: Half-sister of Stowe through Lyman Beecher's two wives
cross_connections.description: Half-sisters by the same father Lyman Beecher — Isabella by his second wife Harriet Porter, Harriet by his first wife Roxanna Foote. Isabella married into the Hooker family; Harriet wrote Uncle Tom's Cabin.

cross_connections.display_label: Nook Farm neighbor — community her family helped build
cross_connections.description: Isabella Beecher Hooker and Mark Twain were Nook Farm neighbors — she and John Hooker had helped establish the community on Farmington Avenue before Twain moved in 1874.

cross_connections.display_label: Half-siblings through Lyman Beecher; both reformers
cross_connections.description: Half-siblings through Lyman Beecher — Edward by Roxanna Foote, Isabella by Harriet Porter. Both reformers; Edward organized Illinois' first anti-slavery society.

cross_connections.display_label: Granddaughter who preserved her Nook Farm legacy
cross_connections.description: Katharine Seymour Day was Isabella's granddaughter — she grew up on Nook Farm surrounded by Isabella's suffragist work and spent her life preserving the community her grandmother helped build.

cross_connections.display_label: Ohio's leading suffragist abolitionist — Hooker descendant
was a later Maryland suffrage leader who also married a Hooker
led New York woman suffrage alongside her
a fellow abolitionist and suffrage orator of the same era

narrative_blocks.header: Read Blackstone — spent decades undoing it
narrative_blocks.body: At nineteen Isabella Beecher married John Hooker and became a regular visitor to his Hartford law office, where she read Blackstone's Commentaries and discovered that under common law marriage suspended a woman's legal standing entirely. She spent the rest of her life undoing it. She died in 1907 at eighty-four, still at work.

narrative_blocks.header: Seven-year fight that won married women property
narrative_blocks.body: Beginning in 1870 Isabella shepherded a married women's property bill through the Connecticut legislature year after year, defeated and reintroduced. It finally passed in 1877, ending the doctrine that a wife's earnings and property automatically belonged to her husband.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Connecticut's first suffrage association in 1869
narrative_blocks.body: Isabella founded the Connecticut Woman Suffrage Association in 1869, the state's first organized suffrage body. Two years later she paid out of her own pocket for a national suffrage convention in Washington, D.C.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Co-founder (1853-1907); Co-founded the community with husband John Hooker; raised 3 children at Forest + Hawthorn.

burialCemetery.name: Cedar Hill Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Major Hartford garden cemetery. Contains graves of J.P. Morgan Sr., Mark Twain, and many prominent Hartford families.
